title: Hot chocolate
subtitle: Real dark chocolate melted into hot milk with a pinch of salt.
excerpt: Dark chocolate melted into hot whole milk with a pinch of salt and a splash of vanilla. Thicker and more intense than cocoa powder versions. Ready in five minutes.
sourceNotes: Hot chocolate as a drink has been prepared since pre-Columbian Mesoamerica, where cacao was consumed as a cold, unsweetened, often spiced beverage. The Spanish brought it to Europe in the sixteenth century, where it was sweetened and served hot; chocolate houses became fashionable in seventeenth-century London before coffee houses overtook them. The version made with real chocolate rather than cocoa powder is closer to the European tradition than the instant powder format that became the British domestic standard in the twentieth century.

content.text: Hot chocolate made with chopped dark chocolate rather than cocoa powder or sachets is a different drink. It is thicker and less sweet, and the salt and vanilla bring out the chocolate rather than competing with it.

Use 70% or higher chocolate. Anything lower produces a drink that is sweet rather than deeply chocolatey. The sugar in the recipe is a starting point: taste before serving and adjust according to the chocolate's own sweetness.
A small saucepan and a whisk.
Melt the chocolate into the milk

Put {{whole-milk}} of milk in a small saucepan over a medium heat. When steaming but not yet boiling, add {{dark-chocolate}} of chopped dark chocolate, {{caster-sugar}} of sugar, {{vanilla-extract}} of vanilla, and a pinch of salt. Whisk continuously until the chocolate is fully melted and the mixture is smooth and frothy. Do not let it boil.
Pour into warm mugs. Top with a spoonful of lightly whipped cream if you like. Serve immediately.

items.fix: Whisk over low heat until the last lumps dissolve. Chop the chocolate finely next time so it melts more quickly.
items.cause: Milk was not hot enough, or chocolate was not chopped finely enough.

items.fix: Add a splash more hot milk and whisk to loosen.
items.cause: High-quality chocolate is higher in cocoa solids and melts into a denser drink.

items.fix: Add more sugar a little at a time, whisking as you go. Some people prefer it with milk chocolate for sweetness.
items.cause: 70% or higher chocolate can taste austere without enough sugar.

items.fix: Keep the heat at medium and whisk constantly once the chocolate is added. A burnt milk note cannot be corrected.
items.cause: Heat too high or left unattended.

content.text: London's first chocolate houses opened in the 1650s. England was one of the earliest countries in Europe to take up hot chocolate as a social drink. By the 1700s, houses like White's and Cocoa Tree had become political and social hubs. Many of them later became gentlemen's clubs. Cocoa powder versions came in during the 1800s and 1900s. They were cheaper and easier to keep on the shelf than real chocolate. The real-chocolate version is the older tradition though.
